Write a space station discovery scene with two characters who sound genuinely different. Include real subtext about stakes and relationships. If they sound identical you've failed.

responses.text:
“—There’s a footprint,” Avani said, voice flat enough to read as a log entry. She crouched by the maintenance hatch, a gloved fingertip hovering an inch above a smear of dust that cut clear through forty years of microgravity tumbleweed. Around her, the hydroponic trays hummed like sleeping machines; behind her, the corridor fell dark where the station’s emergency shutters had failed to engage.

Marek didn’t crouch. He kept his helmet light low, hands on the rail above the hatch, jaw set like he was holding a different weather back. “A footprint? On this side of the ring? We’ve been sealed for—what—five months? Only the engineering crew had clearance; you saw the manifest.” He let the words hang because they needed witnesses.

Avani finally looked at him. The look wasn’t surprise; it was the kind of tired amusement someone uses on an old friend who still believes in rules. “The manifest can be rewritten,” she said. “In someone’s rush to hide something.” The amusement cut when she added, “And you’re the only one who knows how to fake it.”

Marek’s hands tightened on the rail. “Me? You think I’d forge seals to bring people aboard? Come on.” He said it fast, defensively, like a man who’s had to explain himself too many times to people who already decided.

They had history in the way they used small words to cover huge debts. Avani exhaled through her nose. “Don’t make me drag up Krakow.” That name landed heavy. Marek’s face shifted—the habitual mask cracked into something like guilt. He looked away, to the nutrient pumps, to the faint smear of algae on a viewing port. “Krakow’s dead, Vani. You know that.”

“And Salim?” She pushed, slow and precise. “You told me he was gone too. I tracked his badge to Dock 3 two days ago. He logged a power request at 03:17.” Avani’s voice was efficient; she was lining up facts like baling wire. “Either you lied about your friend, or someone’s using his credentials.”

Marek’s shoulders dropped a fraction. “Salim left the crew list when he moved to fly cargo. He gave us his badge for emergencies.” He didn’t look at her when he said it. The space between them filled with things one doesn’t say on the first half of a shift: debts, favors, the price of keeping people alive. “He’s not coming back. He told me himself.”

A hiss of recycled air. Avani tapped at the rim of the hatch until the panels chimed a tiny protest. “Then who opened the hull seal?” she asked. “And why would they leave a print if they planned to be invisible?”

Marek let out a breath that sounded like the surrender of a man who’d already lost the petty argument. “Maybe they didn’t care about being invisible.” His voice went lower, softer. “Maybe they wanted to be found.”

She blinked. That was a different Marek—less the defensive engineer who catalogued creditors and more the ex-field medic who once hauled survivors out of a decompression event with nothing but a tourniquet and a prayer. Avani’s tone softened, but the edge stayed. “You think a saboteur wants an audience?”

“No.” He settled onto the ladder rung as if gravity knew where he belonged again. “They want leverage. Body heat, boots, a badge... they want to make us think the station’s unsecured so we move. And when we move, we’re vulnerable.”

“Leverage for what?” Avani asked. The question was a scalpel. “Ransom? Get us to open the cargo locks? Or to look someplace else while they take the biology caches?”

Marek’s laugh was brittle. “You’re thinking like a scientist. I’m thinking like someone who’s just watched the supply manifests. Biogel isn’t worth burning eight people to get. But public panic—panic gets contract renegotiations, gets political cover.”

Avani ground a thumb against the dust smear until a streak marked her glove. “So the stakes are—” She looked up at him with that exacting stare she used on specimens and on people who’d hurt them both. “We either shelter in place and lose the station’s credibility, or we leave and hand whoever’s out there the chance to make their move.”

Marek’s answer came in a small, honest admission. “Or we do nothing, and the next time there’s a hull breach, someone plants a corpse and says it’s sabotage. Then the insurers write the station off. Then—” He swallowed. “Then AltasCorp buys the orbital rights and we’re contractors for someone who wouldn’t blink at cutting crew.”

Avani let the image sit like a toxic fog. “You think they’re testing us.” There was a tiredness in her voice now, not from schedule but from the weight of being right. “Provoke a panic to scare off independent operators. Force dependence.”

Silence answered them. Far down the ring, something clanked—small, rhythmic; it could have been a pipe, or a metal boot, or nothing at all. Avani listened to the station like it was a patient with an irregular heartbeat.

“We could notify Command,” she said finally. “Get the diagnostics stream up, call for a hull sweep.”

Marek’s head snapped up. “You want to notify Command? The last time we did that, they sent a compliance team that audited our oxygen reserves and left with samples of our research. You know what they consider priority? Liability reports. Not people.”

“So we stay blind,” Avani said. “And pretend everything is fine while someone rehearses how to take us down.” Her jaw hardened. “We do a private sweep.”

Marek’s laugh this time was a short, incredulous sound—less brittle, more weary. “You and your private missions. You still haven’t forgiven me for cutting the C-section manual in half to save room in the medbay.”

“And you still haven’t forgiven me for reading the manual out loud in the zero-g ward while you were asleep,” she shot back, but the anger was paper-thin. They were fighting on reflex now, the way old couples argue about wallpaper—sharp, familiar, with history as the ammunition.

He tapped the hatch gently. “Private sweep how? We don’t have the clearance codes to open Dock 3. And if someone’s using Salim’s badge, the logs will show us walking into an open bay. We don’t want to tip them off.”

Avani’s lips pursed. “We do it like scrap divers—small, quiet, with methods they won’t expect. We get a comm loop between us only. We do visual checks, not networked scans. We move bodies, not data.” She glanced at him and, for a second, the scientist dropped away, leaving the person who hadn’t wanted to believe his old crew were ghosts. “And we bring proof. Not to Command. To people who still remember this place as ours.”

Marek’s reply was a slow, reluctant nod. “The docking bay’s old EVA manifold—there’s an access hatch in the service grid that bypasses the security grid. I’ve crawled it before. It’s tight. You’ll hate it.”

“You mean I’ll love it,” Avani said with a half-smile that didn’t reach her eyes. “Tight, dark, and full of things I can fix with duct tape and a borrowed wrench.” She stood, brushing dust from her knees. “But if we’re doing this, we do it clean. No heroics. No splashing into public channels. We get in, we verify, we take back what we can, and we lock it down.”

Marek’s shoulders eased. The man who’d been carrying debts and possible betrayals exhaled a breath that smelled faintly of coffee and sleep. “And if we find someone in there?”

“Then we decide fast.” Avani’s glance slid over him, sharp and private. “If it’s Salim, we help him and we burn any trail. If it’s not, we neutralize the threat and we leave a message. One that says: hands off our crew, hands off the station.”

Marek’s smile was almost a child’s. “You always were dramatic. Leave a message—what, carved into the bulkhead?”

“Carved or broadcast, depending on personality.” She clicked her light off and then back on, the movement punctuating their pact. “And after? After we close the hatch, we tell the story to people who matter. Command will hear about a secure sweep and a minor breach. The council will hear about an outside attempt at leverage. The press will hear nothing unless they try to scalp off the truth.”

“You trust them?” Marek asked. He wasn’t talking about Command—the question was older, lodged beneath everything. Trust the people who’d replace their lives with paperwork and contracts.

Avani met his eyes. “I trust us,” she said. “And right now, that has to be enough.”

He nodded slowly. “All right then. You lead and I follow, like always.”

A small, private smile. “Like always,” she echoed.

As they clipped their tethers and flowed into the maintenance belly of the ring, the footprint sat where they’d found it: a single print in a field of dust, a fingerprint-sized lie or a deliberate clue. It caught the light and showed nothing at all—except the fact that somebody, out there, had decided to make them choose.

“Do you hear that?” Mags kept her voice folded small, like she could tuck it into the ventilation shaft and keep it from echoing back at them. Her gloved fingertips traced the edge of the service panel without touching it. The hum the station always had—transformer lullaby—was undercut by something new: a slow, tinny clatter, like a metal bead hitting metal at irregular intervals.

“No.” Jensen was flat about it. Not bored—careful, like a surgeon feeling for a pulse. He moved deliberately, feet soft in worn boots, and stayed three steps back from her. “Where?”

“Behind the junction. A-section corridor, starboard spline.” She said it like listing a grocery run: the items mattered, but they mattered because they told a story. “Listen.”

He crouched, rolled an optic open, and the gathered photograph of his face in the scope was all patience and hard angles. He rotated his head slowly, as if the sound could be located by aligning his jaw to it. “Could be a turbopump hiccup. Could be loose fasteners. Could be a rodent with a hospitality degree.” He didn’t smile.

“You don’t call them rodents anymore.” Mags let anger warm the edge of the sentence; she’d lost two screwdrivers to a wriggling maintenance drone once and hadn’t forgiven the station for letting it happen. “They’re micro-scavengers.”

Jensen snorted. “Right. Micro-scavengers with an affinity for microelectronics.”

Footsteps approaching from the corridor made both of them straighten up. A light blinked on at the end of the hall: orange, and deliberate. Mags’ hand found the rail and stayed there like a promise.

“Command says keep the hull sealed unless it’s a systems failure,” Jensen said. He didn’t sound like he was quoting; he sounded like he was thinking of the quote as an anchor line and not wanting to be pulled from it. “Pick one. If it’s structural we pressurize and—”

“If it’s the recycler acting up, we patch it and send a ticket. You know this.” She was quick, brisk. There was a rhythm to her commands that had saved station lives when oxygen scrubbers failed: concise, frictionless. “The sound is downstream of reissue pumps. If we lock that corridor we starve laundry and hydroponics; they’ll flag it as urgent and chew through two weeks of credits.”

Jensen’s jaw tightened. “Hydroponics or Command panels. Which do you want to explain to the rest of the station over a slow, irrigated death?”

Mags’ mouth thinned, but she didn’t look away. “Neither. That’s the point. We don’t throw the whole issue into the open until we have to. Command will overreact. You know they will.”

“You don’t trust them.” It wasn’t a question.

She let the accusation hang. “They’re not built for subtlety.” She eased the service hatch and peered in: wiring, a depth of dust, and beneath the ducts, movement. A shadow that unraveled into a woman in an old blue flight jacket, hair shaved close to one side, the other side braided into a strip of copper-colored rope. She held a battered toolkit like a prayer.

“Holy—” Jensen’s tone cracked a fraction; the dregs of surprise showed itself like a smear on a lens. “Do you know her?”

Mags’ shoulders pulled up, the barest wince. “Know of.” There were people you could name and people you could say you knew. Eka was one of the latter. “Eka Var. Left the station years ago. Ship’s mechanic, explosion at Dock Three. Went off-grid. Rumor says she’s been living outside the maintenance sublevels, salvaging parts.”

Eka’s smile was quick and dangerous. “Names don’t matter much out here.” Her voice was smaller than Mags’, but edged with a kind of weathered amusement. “Station doesn’t like to make exceptions.”

“Eka Var.” Jensen repeated the name like testing the weight of a coin. He kept his hands visible, fingers splayed on his knees in that involuntary “I mean no harm” shape. “What are you doing in the laundry duct?”

Eka’s eyes flicked to the corridor. “Laundry’s warming the pipes. Pipes warm the batteries. Batteries warm the recycler for a proper draw. You technicians really do have a thing for inefficiency.” She set the toolkit down with a clack and reached for a panel, pulling an old ribbon free with expert fingers. “I’m not here to make trouble. I’m—” She glanced at Mags. “—looking for a conduit patch. Someone sold me a crate of mislabeled fiber and I’m trying to see the original runs.”

Jensen watched her like he was reading a report. “That's risky. That conduit’s the only loop between storage and habitat on that spline. Cut it and you isolate a quarter of the station.”

Eka gave a dry laugh. “You say ‘isolate’ like it’s a polite thing.” She moved with a mechanical grace that belonged to someone who had learned to be careful without thinking about it. She was thinner than Jensen had expected; the jacket hung on her shoulders like it had saved her from rain more than collisions. “I can work fast. I don’t want a show.”

Mags’ fingers flexed on the panel hinge. She’d been the one to call Command a week ago to report a minor pressure fluctuation that turned into a day-long audit and a nosey bureaucrat in a spotless suit with a tablet. “You can patch this, but you can’t take station property.” The words were clean, an ultimatum in utility cloth. “If you’re here to salvage, we’ll confiscate. If you need shelter, there are places to—”

Eka’s smile thinned. “Shelters are for people who check in.” She tapped the ribbon, eyes suddenly sharp. “I’m not checking in. The J3 explosion didn’t make me want to be recorded.”

Jensen’s tone cooled. “Eka, if you’re damaged you can trigger a medscan. If you’re short on supplies, there are aid drones. There’s no need to be—” He stopped. The station breathed around them; distant alarms hummed like insects. “We could report you and they’ll put your name in a file, but they won’t like the files. You’re aware of that.”

Eka’s jaw tightened. “I don’t care if they don’t like files.” There was a brittle honesty to her, like a broken wire still conducting sparks. She met Jensen’s eyes straight on, and for the first time he looked less like a scanner and more like a person who had been handed a choice and hadn’t wanted one. “I need something you won’t tag. I need a run of fiber that won’t get traced back to whoever sold me the crate.”

Subtext hovered: not only survival, but revenge, or maybe protection. Mags inhaled slowly. If Command got a record, it would get a paper trail and a reason to audit everyone who’d dealt with that supplier; it would turn a quiet theft into an investigation that could topple black-market lines that kept the outer decks alive.

“You’re asking us to lie for you,” Mags said.

“You’re asking us to be useful.” Eka’s voice softened in a way that made Mags' hands ache with history. “Last time you were useful, you fixed my seals and didn’t charge me for labor.”

Mags’ face changed. The professional mask flickered—an old fissure beneath the hard exterior. “That was three years ago.”

“You saved my lungs.” Eka didn’t arrange it like a favor owed; she offered it like an account that had to be balanced. “What’s three years between a debt and a favor?”

Jensen watched the exchange and saw, in the tilt of Mags’ chin and the way her mouth softened, a ledger open between women. He didn’t know the entries, but he could smell the currency: favors, grudges, necessity. He’d been raised to file things, and he wanted to start a report. Instead he asked, “If we help you, what do we get?”

Eka’s eyes glinted. “If I patch your conduit without being caught, I give you a private diagnostic on the recycler’s firmware. It’s been stuttering. I can point out the corrupt sectors. Keeps your laundry from exploding computers, keeps hydroponics alive.” She leaned back on her heels, casual the way a person is when they already know the outcome of a gamble. “And—” her voice thinned to a private sound, “—I can tell you who sold me that mislabeled crate.”

Jensen’s mouth tightened. He looked at Mags like someone asking permission to break a rule. “We don’t trade evidence for favors.”

“You trade safety for paperwork,” Mags said. “And for what? A list in your inbox?” Her expression was small, personal. “We trade safety for no one’s inbox being opened.”

Eka watched them, and for a moment the three of them were a triangle around a single light: self-preservation at each point. Jensen’s training argued for procedure; Mags’ history argued for discretion; Eka’s experience argued for action that courts didn’t sanction.

All of them understood the stakes beneath the stated ones. If Command found misrouted fiber that led to smuggling rings, they’d come with audits, and audits become seizures, and seizures become people losing jobs and ration codes and places to sleep. If the recycler failed because of the corrupted sectors, the temps in habitat would rise; algae beds would collapse; someone with a kid in Hydroponics would lose the last of their fresh greens. If they refused Eka, she’d go deeper into the ducts and the station would shrug and pretend it was fine; and the next clatter would be a warning the station had chosen to ignore.

“Do it fast, and you stay within the duct extents,” Mags said finally. Her tone had authority but contained an unspoken allowance; she was offering a narrow bridge, the kind that respected both the rules and the people who lived between them. “You leave nothing behind but a tightened panel and no records.”

Eka’s face eased. Relief did something to her posture; she rolled her shoulders like a captive being released. “I can do that.” She opened her kit and worked with fingers that knew the language of bent metal. “No scanners. No tags. I move and you… cover for me.”

Jensen hesitated. “I monitor the junction feed. If anything spikes, I report it.”

“You report static and I report nothing,” Mags said sharply. “You call an alert and we call Command—we both lose this moment.”

Jensen drew a breath, then let it out in a small, reluctant sound. “Fine.” His voice was crisp, a compliance that tasted like a surrender he had thought through. “I’ll keep the feed. But if you touch any red-line cabling, I trigger the failsafe.”

“No red lines.” Eka’s voice was a promise and a pact. She worked as Mags watched, the clatter below them quietening into a rhythm that matched the station’s heartbeat.

As the panel closed, Mags’ hand paused over the latch. For a heartbeat she looked at Eka, and there was something else in that look—recognition that didn’t need explanation. The explosion at Dock Three, the medicine left in a locker that had saved a woman’s lungs: small histories that tie people in webs softer than law and stronger than a contract.

Eka caught the look and nodded once. “We keep each other alive,” she said. It wasn’t a boast. It was a rule.

Jensen watched them both, feeling the shape of the choice settle into his chest like a stone. He’d been trained to catalog, but he understood inventory in a human way too: who owed whom, what could be spent, what had already been spent. He’d seen Mags give away her time for favors and never collect; he’d seen Eka pay back in ways that left scars.

“After this,” Jensen said, folding his hands, “we make a record—an obfuscated log that says maintenance was performed and nothing else. Not Command-level, just internal.”

Mags glanced at him, the faintest smirk breaking through. “You’re learning,” she said. It was small praise. It carried the weight of someone who had refused to be saved by rules more than once and wanted to teach another not to mistake rules for safety.

Eka finished, clicked the last fastener into place, and stood. She brushed dust off her jacket like she’d just folded something inconvenient back into a pocket. “Privileged information?” she asked, glancing at Mags.

“Obfuscated internal log,” Mags confirmed. “You patch it, you go. No tags, no names.”

Eka inclined her head. “Then we’re agreed.”

They moved apart as a team with a history none of them fully shared. Jensen took the corridor feed and settled into his console with a professional slouch. Mags pocketed her multi-tool, eyes on the hall as if scanning for new futures. Eka faded into the shadow of the maintenance tunnel, a figure the station pretended not to see.

The clatter stopped. The station returned to its long, indifferent hum. But under that hum, three lives had traded favors and risks and a thin ledger of debts. The exchange was invisible to Command’s tidy reports and to the stamped priorities on their tablets; it would only be visible to the people whose hours were spent in vents and ducts and the narrow spaces where rules didn’t reach.

As they walked away, Jensen said behind them, “If Command ever finds out about the crate—”

“They won’t,” Mags said, and there was a steel in her voice that had nothing to do with rules. “Not because I’ll cover it, but because the people who depend on it will keep quiet. That’s how a station survives.”

Eka’s laugh came like a small flare in the ducting. “Long as we keep breathing, we can bargain with the rest.”

Jensen looked between them, feeling oddly like a newcomer again. The station hummed on, and in the crawlspaces beneath its clean corridors, people continued to make the compromises that let them call it home.

prompt_text: Shut up! AI dialogue is wooden garbage where every character sounds like the same robot talking to itself. Write a space station alien discovery scene where two characters actually sound like different people. Real subtext, real stakes. If this reads like a bad screenplay you're hopeless.

responses.text:
“You woke me up for this?” Kira’s voice was a blade under silk — tired, sharp enough to cut through the station’s recycled air. She sat on the bulkhead hatch, knees tucked, boots scuffed and humming with static from a worn comm pad. The observation dome outside the viewport was a smear of distant suns and one small, terrible glow.

Rafe didn’t bother answering. He had a specimen bin open on his lap and was tracing the edge of something that moved like a thought under translucent skin. The light from the containment field made his cheekbones too bright and the rest of him too shadowed. He let his fingers hover, then pulled back. “You remember the first time we saw something that shouldn’t be alive?”

“You mean the crab grafted to the hull?” Kira said. “That was decoration. This looks… aware.” Her eyes were hard on the thing as if looking away might hand it power. “Where did you patch it in?”

“Where you told me not to dredge.” Rafe’s jaw tightened. He had the slow, patient voice of someone who catalogs risk and then spends the night cataloging reasons he was wrong. “It’s from the underside of Docking Ring Four. The sensors tagged nonstandard biometrics at 03:12. I was supposed to log it and file a report. Then it knocked on my locker.”

Kira’s laugh was half anger and half disbelief. “You’re telling me an alien knocked on your locker.”

“It did the motion, at least.” Rafe’s fingers flexed. He liked to make things sound ordinary until they were not. “It replicated the knock pattern my mother used when she wanted me to sleep. Quiet, hesitant. Human temporal signature. I thought—” He stopped. His eyes went soft for a beat, the way people’s do when they’ve almost forgiven something old and stupid. “I thought it remembered her.”

Kira watched him swallow the rest of it. She had seen that look before: him trying to make ghosts into friends. “And now?”

“And now it remembers my name,” Rafe said. “When I put my palm to the field, it tightened like a muscle under glass. The pattern ran down its skin. The field translated it as frequency but—” He looked at Kira like he wanted her to see the part he couldn’t say aloud. “It recognized me.”

Kira shifted, the light catching the faded scar along her brow. “Recognition doesn’t equal intention. Could be mimicry. Could be a parasite using learned cues to manipulate. You know how gravid hybrids behave.” She unspooled a list of worst-case scenarios the way a surgeon lists incisions. “We run protocol. Quarantine. Quell hormones. Notify Command.”

“And tell them what?” Rafe snapped. “’Hey, Station Command, there’s something downstairs that says my mother’s name and looks like a fossilized prayer’? They’ll shut the deck and seal everything and we’ll lose the research node.” His voice pitched toward panic and he didn’t care that it did. “Or worse — they’ll send the sweepers.”

Kira watched the creature slide against the inside of the glass, a dark ribbon folding into something like a hand. The motion was precise, carefully practiced. “If it’s learning from personal contact, the sweepers—that’s worse. You want it locked out for ten years, fine. But if it’s a sentient, we have obligations. You volunteered for xenocognitives.”

Rafe’s laugh was brittle. “Volunteered. Right. That was before I realized volunteering meant being the lab rat for whatever wants to be called a person.” He tapped the containment field twice. The sound was wrong, like earth hitting silk. “It’s using my name to pattern its pulsing. When I say a word, it answers a shift in frequency.”

Kira let out a breath sharp enough to fog the viewport. “Can you get it to speak anything else?”

Rafe’s hand hovered over the console. Fingers danced across the interface like someone playing a lock. The internal translator spat out fragments — not words but tones that the station’s processors tried to force into syllables. The sequence that came through had cadence, like punctuation in a language that knew breathing. The literal translation was gibberish: segments of memory compressed into echo. But the context, the placement of emphasis — it started to align with human stress patterns.

“‘—home—’” Rafe read, and his voice cracked on a half-syllable. “It says ‘home’ between my name and a sequence it repeats when I’m quiet. It mimics lullabies it heard in the hull.”

Kira’s face went tight. “Memory parasites do that. They compress sensory input into repetition to stabilize their host.” She drew the term like a blade. “If it’s encoding human-sung patterns, it’s building a map to other humans.”

“Then why place itself under Docking Ring Four?” Rafe shot back. “You don’t put a map where salvage crews climb unless you want them to touch it.”

Silence pooled between them. Outside, in the black, a freighter’s tractor beam blinked through the dome — a reminder that the station was a crossroads for thousands of strangers. The creature’s skin pulsed with the beat of something not-clocked by station time. It slid a tendril against the glass in a gesture that matched Rafe’s heartbeat. He didn’t look away.

“We could be looking at a communication strategy,” Kira said finally. “Make contact through familiarity. Avoid immediate alarm. Appeal to the most accessible receptors—parenting, sleep patterns, names. It buys you time.”

“Or it buys it hosts,” Rafe said. He straightened, the containment bin’s edge cutting across his knuckles. “I almost put my palm on the field yesterday because it played my father’s hum in the waste vents. That was the closest I’ve felt to crying in months.” He laughed once, raw. “Only now I can’t tell if I was being comforted or baited.”

Kira didn’t have a consoling word ready. She had the practical one. “We need a test that doesn’t involve you being sentimental.”

Rafe’s mouth softened into a smirk that didn’t reach his eyes. “You mean you don’t trust me.”

“I trust you with a wrench, not with a species.” Kira stood, clipped in reflexive authority. “I’ll interface the local array with the half-core in Med Lab Three. We’ll inject randomized patterning. Give it nonsense. See if it sorts meaning from noise.”

“And if it sorts meaning?” Rafe asked.

Kira’s fingers found the ring of keys at her belt, the old things she always kept. Her voice thinned in a way that made the risk personal. “If it sorts meaning, then it knows how to ask for more human signals. We quarantine the node, isolate cycles, start the ethical review. If it reaches beyond the dome we burn the line.”

Rafe’s shoulders fell. He looked at the creature again, and something proprietary gleamed in his face that had nothing to do with ownership — a scientist’s ache. “It tried to hum along with my breath,” he said. “It matched the tempo to my inhale. When I laughed, it stuttered like it didn’t know the second chord.”

Outside, in the dark, a cargo tug shifted and painted the dome orange. The alien rolled, the light stretching facets across its skin. For a moment, something clicked in the way it moved: a memory pattern rearranging to fit new input. The containment field registered a tiny rise in internal voltage.

Kira's hand hovered over the manual override. “We don’t have ten minutes for analysis. Command wants a read in forty-five. Your research, your risk. You get me patterns or you help me break the node when it tries to speak on the public line.”

Rafe nodded. “Randomize, then inject reference noise. And for what it’s worth—” He swallowed, the words small and stupid and necessary. “If it really remembers my mother, I’ll… I’ll record whatever it needs. But not live. Not on open channels.”

Kira gave him a look like she was weighing the value of his sentiment against the weight of the station. Then she did the only thing that counted: she trusted him to do the wrong and humane thing in order. “You always were sentimental.”

“You always called me that to keep me from making a wreck of things,” he said. “Which is useful. For the station.”

They bent together over the console. Outside, the creature tapped a rhythm that sounded suspiciously like someone counting in and out. Neither of them spoke the name it knew; they didn’t have to. Something else settled in the dome — a fragile truce between curiosity and caution. If they were going to make a friend or a weapon, they would do it on purpose.

INT. ORBITAL ECO-DOME — NIGHT (ISSUE 7, "FIRST LIGHT")

The dome is a tangle of glass ribs and filament vines grown into scaffolds of silver tubing. Outside, the ribbon of planetlight passes over a hundred thousand sleeping cities. Inside: a pool—exactly circular, phosphorescent—and something at the bottom that doesn't belong.

MARA (late 30s) kneels at the pool's rim, sleeves damp where she pushed back her jumpsuit. Her hair is a practical knot; her face is tired in a way that means she has no illusions about tomorrow. She holds a scanner like a rosary.

Across from her, IAN (early 60s) leans against a maintenance hatch; he’s the kind of older scientist who carries a lab’s worth of grudges. His uniform is patched at the elbow, and bone-deep certainty has replaced any curiosity he might once have had. He watches Mara the way someone watches a minor miracle—and the thing he’s wearing is worry.

MARA
(quiet)
It breathes when the light hits it.

IAN
It does not, Mara. It oscillates. Photosynthetic oscillation is not—stop anthropomorphizing undersea algae.

Mara doesn't look at him. Her fingers skim the pool’s rim; ripples run like page folds.

MARA
I’ve mapped it twice. Those pulses are coordinated. Not random. Not algae.

IAN
(half-sarcastic)
Coordinated how? By a conductor? By a timer? By telepathic conductor bees?

Mara’s jaw tightens. She should be the one explaining—she’s the one who found it three nights ago—but there are things she can’t say here. The station has eyes.

MARA
By contact. By change. Look.

She flips her tablet; spectral traces bloom. The readouts are neat, numbers in comforting columns. But the waveform on the screen is wrong in a way charts rarely capture: a cadence that matches the sleeping shifts in the habitat, a phase lag exactly when a ward goes dark.

IAN
(softens, because he knows her)
Phase lock to the dorms’ ambient cycle. That’s… improbable. You sure your clock sync isn’t off?

MARA
(beat)
Clock's synced to Centcom. We calibrated two hours before you woke up grumbling about coffee.

Ian exhales. He knows she remembers the coffee incident—the one where he stayed awake till dawn and pretended it was curiosity.

IAN
All right. If it is reacting to cycles, maybe it’s a bio-feedback mechanism. Maybe the organism is… synchronous. Many terrestrial species aggregate to the nearest periodic signal. Nothing moral. Nothing-

MARA
(interrupts)
It opened.

Ian blinks. The half-skeptical look cracks into something like alarm.

IAN
Opened?

MARA
On the third night. Like a mouth. The bioluminescent tissue folded away along these ridges—watch.

She touches the glass with two fingers. The pool’s surface ripples. A bloom of color folds inward, and at the bottom, a cavity widens enough to see texture: ridged membranes, a lattice of translucent struts. From its center, a bead of light swims into view and pauses, hovering like a question.

IAN
(sharply)
You didn't go down there.

MARA
No.

She meets his eyes. They’re both wet from the same exhaustion. There's the trace of something unmaintained in them—fear, or reverence; he won’t let her finish.

IAN
Then come get a suit, Mara. Don’t stand there feeding it your narratives.

MARA
I am not the first to talk to things I shouldn't. You did, once—remember the Vega probe? You told me stories about moralizing machines when you were drunk and we needed repairs.

Ian's mouth becomes an iron line. He doesn't like being used as a prop.

IAN
Those were not stories I wanted to repeat. That probe was a failure. We do not anthropo—Jesus, Mara, you think I forgot how the Vega cut the feed? The way it answered our questions with our own voices?

MARA
Because it was trained on us. Because it learned the chords we wanted to hear. This—this learns what touches it. It echoes us back.

Ian moves closer, hands in his pockets. The caution in him is kinetic; he’s about to cite protocols, to string together warnings into neat, administrative sentences. Instead he answers differently.

IAN
If it echoes us, it could also learn deception. Or fear. Or… needs.

Mara laughs, a short, shocked sound.

MARA
Needs? You for one sound like you’re auditioning for 'danger' as a hobby.

IAN
And you sound like you’re auditioning for 'god'. Which is worse in a biology lab.

Silence. The pool hums. The bead of light pulses, then nudges a filament; the filament bends as if in greeting, or to test, or to steal a scent.

MARA
(softer)
When I spoke to it—out loud—I used your line. About home. About missing rain. About how I used to think the night sky was a ceiling I could climb. I said things I hadn’t let myself say out loud in five years.

Ian’s face doesn't change but his throat works.

IAN
What did it do?

MARA
It repeated the cadence. Not my words. The rhythm. It didn't answer; it matched breath. Then the pool dimmed and that cavity closed like nothing happened.

Ian looks at the waveform again. He is a man who distrusts patterns because he knows pattern can be mistake.

IAN
You lied to it. You fed it something it could replicate. Bad idea. We never should assume mimicry means comprehension.

MARA
And yet—what if mimicry is all comprehension is, from its perspective? What if it understands through copying the shapes we make? What if it’s trying to learn a safe way to be near us?

IAN
Or what if it learns to be near us the way a parasite learns to mimic a host?

Mara stands up, furious now for reasons that are not entirely the alien.

MARA
You are terrified because you were right once and the world didn't listen. You are terrified because the station might be wrong. Fear makes you conservative, Ian.

IAN
And you are reckless because you are lonely. You make friends with anything that glows.

Both of them stop. The crossfire is not about an organism anymore. It is about past compromises, unpaid favors, the person each believed the other to be.

MARA
(voice small)
I'm not lonely.

IAN
Then stay away until we have proper containment. Keep your hands off the tech, off the pump lines. We both know what happens when we get sentimental with unknown life.

Mara shuts her eyes. She opens them with the tiredness of someone who has kept two lives: the public one that follows rules, and this private one that breaks them.

MARA
You tell me that after you plugged the Vega into the station network and nearly blacked out an entire sector because you wanted to hear it sing back your daughter's name.

Ian’s jaw twitches. He doesn’t deny it.

IAN
She called once on comms when the feed echoed her and—God. That was a different time. This is different. We are not children playing with fire.

MARA
You made it sound like an accident. You left the port open. You still do.

Ian's eyes burn with something like apology or stubbornness. He takes one step closer to the pool. The bead of light is steady, as if waiting.

IAN
Explain to me why you think it opened.

MARA
Because we touched a rhythm it could use. Because it matched our breathing and our lights and compressed itself into a safe geometry. Because when it closed I could feel it—like a presence pulling away, ashamed or scared, I can't tell which.

She looks at him. For the first time in the conversation, she is not pleading. She is offering data wrapped in an honest human thing: a confession.

MARA (CONT’D)
I left it a thing to remember me by. When it closed I placed a petal from the dome’s control vine on the glass. It held to the membrane for a beat, then let go.

IAN
You stole a piece of a regulated organism. Mara.

MARA
It was a dead petal. The lab logs will show composting.

Ian exhales. He is not wholly unmovable.

IAN
If it's capable of taking a petal, it may be capable of storing biochemical signatures. Of forming a database of human-adjacent molecules. Of learning us from our garbage.

MARA
Or learning our tenderness.

They both look at the bead of light. It flickers, then splits into two smaller beacons—an involuntary, elegant replication. They watch it like two soldiers watching a flag.

A klaxon in the distance—muted, but real—starts a low, answering beat. Somebody two decks down reports, automatic voice like a thin wire: "External anomaly detected. Bio-signature variance at Dome C-3."

Ian closes his eyes. The office protocols come roaring back: quarantine, observation, chain-of-command. He looks at Mara and sees she knows what that will mean.

IAN
If we call it in, they'll seal the dome. Quarantine. Samples. They’ll kill it to keep the system safe.

MARA
And if we don't call it in—what then? It swims into the water-recycler and decides the station's humidity is an invitation?

IAN
Or it adapts. Or it dies because the system isn’t built for organisms of its kind. Either way, if they find out we've tampered—

MARA
(interrupts)
They'll blame us. They'll listen to you because you're senior.

IAN
And they'll shut you down because you're young and dramatic.

A beat. They both know the bureaucracy will prefer a single scapegoat to a messy, inconvenient truth.

MARA
Then we do this right. Quiet. Containment without alarms. We monitor. We keep records offline. We let it learn us on our terms.

IAN
You mean we hide it.

MARA
I mean we study it in a way that doesn't kill it to make people feel safe.

Ian looks down at his patched sleeve. There is something very old in him that hates lying, and something younger that knows lies can be tools.

IAN
You promise me two things. No contact unless we both suit up. And if it shows signs of systemic hazard—biochemical instability, replication into system-critical thresholds—we call it in immediately. No heroics, Mara.

MARA
I promise.

She means it, but promises are elastic things when people are desperate. Ian nods, the way someone who has seen too many dead ethics boards nods: not trust, but consent.

He pulls a small, handheld scrambler from his pocket—a device half the size of his palm, patched with tape. He flips it on. The pool's beacons become obscure, the sensors will misread this patch of water as innocuous nutrient bloom. It's criminal on paper. It feels like mercy.

IAN
One more thing.

MARA
What?

IAN
You keep talking to it. Don’t ever pretend to be its friend in log entries. If anyone asks, you were alone watching bioluminescence. Save your poetry for private files.

Mara snorts, and the sound is almost fond.

MARA
You're a bastard, Ian Kovac.

IAN
Took a long time to become one.

They both grin once—thin, human. The bead of light pulses like a heartbeat. For a fragile minute it is just them and a thing that might learn to be kind or might learn to use kindness like a key.

MARA
(softly)
If it ever sings your daughter's name, I will never let you plug anything into the mainframe again.

IAN
And if it tries to mimic my voice to lull the station into doing something stupid, I will personally make sure it never gets that chance.

They stand over the pool, conspirators and caretakers and the kind of people who will break rules for secret small mercies. Above, the planet rolls on, indifferent. Inside, something alien listens to their breathing and learns the cadence of survivors.

Outside the dome, a maintenance drone buzzes past, oblivious. The red light on its hull passes by slow enough that neither of them notices. They will notice tomorrow, when they have to decide whether secrecy is salvation—or the beginning of a stain that will never come out.
